texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas separation membranes have the potential to recovery and purify helium from natural gas. This can be through direct separation of helium from methane or as part of a natural gas liquefaction process where  helium  is  recovered  from  the  nitrogen  rejection  unit  (NRU)  exit  gas.  Here,  membrane  gas separation process simulations are undertaken in Aspen HYSYS to determine the required membrane selectivity and process conditions needed to perform these separations. It was determined that two and three membranes in series processes can separate helium directly from natural gas utilizing currently available polymeric membrane selectivities, if the helium composition in the feed is greater than 1 mol% and at high pressure. However, for lower quality natural gas fields' improvement in He/CH 4 selectivity is required  for  membrane  separation  to  be  viable.  The  recovery  of  helium  from  the  NRU  exit  gas  and concentration  to  70  mol%  is  also  possible  through  two  or  three  membranes  in  series  process  with  a He/N2 selectivity above 5. However, this selectivity requires a significantly high pressure driving force across the membrane stages, because the process is operated at very low stage-cuts. A He/N2 selectivity greater  than  25  enables  recovery  of  helium  and  concentration  to  70  mol%  with  significantly  lower pressure driving force. The upgrading of helium from 70 mol% to 90 mol%, before final purification, is the  most  viable  separation  that  can  be  undertaken  with  existing  gas  separation  membranes  and  the energy  duty  of  a  two  membranes  in  series  process  is  comparable  with  existing  technology.  Hence, current polymeric membranes for helium separation can perform many of the process stages in helium recovery and purification. However, improvements in polymeric membranes' He selectivity enables the various  separation  stages  to  become  increasingly  viable  when  compared  with  existing  separation technologies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Helium is a high value product desired for its chemical inertness, lighter than air property and ability to achieve  cryogenic  temperatures  near  absolute  zero.  As  such,  helium  has  found  application  in  a  wide range of industries, in electronics and fiber optics fabrication, weather balloons and airships, as a heat transfer agent in rocket technology and in cryogenic applications such as medical MRI scanners [1]. The vast majority of helium is sourced from natural gas fields, where it can be concentrated as high as 4 mol% (New Mexico, USA), but on average commercial fields are between 0.2 and 0.5 mol% [2-4]. Recent increases in the helium price because of increasing demand [5] have made previously uneconomic fields attractive and hence there is increasing interest in low cost separation technologies that can recovery helium.

As  such,  helium  has  found  application  in  a  wide range of industries, in electronics and fiber optics fabrication, weather balloons and airships, as a heat transfer agent in rocket technology and in cryogenic applications such as medical MRI scanners [1]. The vast majority of helium is sourced from natural gas fields, where it can be concentrated as high as 4 mol% (New Mexico, USA), but on average commercial fields are between 0.2 and 0.5 mol% [2-4]. Recent increases in the helium price because of increasing demand [5] have made previously uneconomic fields attractive and hence there is increasing interest in low cost separation technologies that can recovery helium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Traditional helium separation from natural gas is achieved in a three stage process, producing purity of 99% or higher. These stages consist of the initial recovery of helium from natural gas, helium upgrading to ~70% and the final helium purification (Figure 1). Helium recovery from natural gas is integrated with nitrogen  removal  through  cryogenic  distillation,  known  as  the  nitrogen  rejection  unit  (NRU)  [1,  6]. Dependent on the particular natural gas field, the exit gas from the NRU has helium at concentrations of 1 - 3 %, with the remainder nitrogen, oxygen and hydrogen. Helium upgrading can be achieved through cryogenic condensation to remove the majority of components and leaving helium in the gas phase. The final  stage  of  helium  purification  is  achieved  through  catalyzed  oxidation  of  hydrogen  and  molecular sieve pressure swing adsorption (PSA) [1, 6, 7]; removing trace amounts of nitrogen and hydrogen. The resulting product then undergoes liquefaction for transport.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For many of these helium recovery process stages, polymeric membrane gas separation is a potential alternative separation technology. This is because the majority of polymeric membranes have selectivity for helium, and the conditions within natural gas and the subsequent separation stages favor a strong helium driving force across a membrane. Only the final helium purification stage to achieve very high purity is poorly suited for membrane separation, because of the presence of hydrogen [7]. Indeed, one of  the  first  applications  trialled  for  gas  separation  membrane  technology was the  recovery of  helium from natural gas [8], though the poor selectivity of membranes at the time meant this separation was not  feasible.  Currently,  a  number  of  commercial  membrane  processes  exist  that  are  marketed  for helium recovery [9-11]. However, their market penetration is minor and associated with niche industries

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here,  simulated  studies  of  membrane  gas  separation  for  helium  recovery  and  purification  are investigated and analyzed for their potential to replace other separation technologies for a number of process stages in helium separation. These stages are the direct separation and concentration of helium from natural gas; initial upgrading of the exit stream from the NRU to 70% helium and further purifying to 90% for final PSA purification (Figure 1). These are achieved through membrane stages in series with recycle streams, with the number of membrane stages dependent on the process as well as feed and exit stream conditions (Figure 2). The focus of the investigation is to determine the necessary helium selectivity required to achieve the degree of separation and purification in each process; and how this is dependent on the concentration of helium in the feed, the pressure driving force across the membrane stages  as  well  as  the  number  of  membrane  stages.  These  determined  helium  selectivities  represent targets needed for membrane gas separation to be considered as a potential alterative technology in helium  recovery.  Other  membrane  process  parameters,  such  as  stage-cut,  are  important  but  the membranes'  selectivity  must  be  above  a  certain  value  for  a  process  to  achieve  the  desired  product purity  and  recovery,  irrespective  of  other  process  parameters.    As  such,  these  selectivity  targets  are compared for existing polymeric membranes that have been reported for helium separation to evaluate their  potential.  Helium  permeability  is  another  important  parameter,  as  in  the  processes  studied  it strongly  correlates  with  required  membrane  area.  In  addition,  the  energy  duty  of  the  membrane separation,  and  other  ancillary  parameters,  are  reported  to  enable  comparison  with  existing  helium separation technologies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Methodology
texts.text: 2. Methodology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All  membrane  simulations  were  undertaken  in  Aspen  HYSYS  (7.5),  using  the  Peng-Robinson  fluid package. The membrane simulations used an in-house module specifically designed for gas separation processes, based on mass transfer equations for cross-flow and counter-flow configurations [13-15]. The membrane  separation  process  is  broken  down  into  100  discrete  stages  and  the  mass  balance  is determined iteratively for each stage.
texts.text: All  membrane  simulations  were  undertaken  in  Aspen  HYSYS  (7.5),  using  the  Peng-Robinson  fluid package. The membrane simulations used an in-house module specifically designed for gas separation processes, based on mass transfer equations for cross-flow and counter-flow configurations [13-15]. The membrane  separation  process  is  broken  down  into  100  discrete  stages  and  the  mass  balance  is determined iteratively for each stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All  membranes  were  operated  in  a  cross-flow  configuration  without  the  use  of  a  sweep  gas,  and assumed to operate at 35 o C. A pressure loss of 0.01 MPa was assumed for the retentate stream across the  membrane.  Compressor  units  were  operated  using  default  Aspen  parameters  and  settings. Therefore  compressors  are  centrifugal  with  an  adiabatic  efficiency  of  75%.  Heat  exchangers  have  an assumed pressure drop of 0.04 MPa with the outlet temperatures set by the operator; gas flow through piping is assumed to have negligible temperature and pressure losses. Membrane processes consisted of one membrane stage, two membrane stages with recycle and three membrane stages with two recycles (Figure 2). For the two membrane stages process, the permeate from the first membrane stage is the feed for the second membrane stage, and the retentate from the second membrane stage is recycled back to the feed of the first membrane stage to ensure high recovery, with the second membrane stage permeate the product stream. For the three membrane stages process, the second membrane stage permeate  stream  becomes  the  feed  for  the  third  membrane  stage,  with  the  third  membrane  stage retentate being recycled back to the second membrane stage feed. Additional compressors and coolers were included when necessary to generate the pressure driving force across each membrane stage and ensure the membranes operated at temperature. All membrane process configurations are designed to meet the respective purity targets in the product streams, for two and three membrane units in series the overall process is also designed to achieve 99 % recovery of the helium in the feed. The membranes' stage-cuts, the fraction of the feed stream that permeates the membrane, are a process variable used to optimise the process in terms of membrane selectivity and process energy duty, the required energy per mass of He separated.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 Helium separation from natural gas
texts.text: 3.1 Helium separation from natural gas

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A range of polymeric membranes have been reported for the helium - methane gas pair [8, 16-19], and the Robeson's plot is provided in Figure 3. The majority of polymeric membranes display selectivity for helium against methane, because their permeability is diffusivity dominated; favoring helium because of its smaller kinetic diameter (2.6 Å relative to 3.8 Å for CH4)  [20]. Some rubbery polymeric membranes display selectivity for methane because solubility dominates their permeability; with helium having very low  solubility  in  all  polymeric  membranes  because  of  its  chemical  inertness.  The  upper  bound  is provided to highlight the current limitation for this gas separation pair, with the gradient corresponding to theory [21].

texts.orig: 3.1.1 Direct helium recovery from natural gas
texts.text: 3.1.1 Direct helium recovery from natural gas

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The direct recovery of helium from natural gas through a membrane process requires a product purity of >99 %, and a recovery of 99%, to be a viable alternative compared with traditional separation process, irrespective of the feed helium composition. A one membrane stage may be able to obtain the purity required, but at the expense of recovery. Hence, a two membrane stages process is studied, and the He/CH4 selectivity required to achieve 99% purity in the product stream from natural gas is provided in Figure  4,  as  a  function  feed  helium  concentration  and  total  pressure  across  the  membrane.  The  two membranes process compresses the first membrane stage permeate stream as the feed to the second membrane, as well as recycling the second membrane stage retentate back to the first membrane feed. The  modeling  demonstrates  that  as  helium  concentration  in  the  feed  increases  the  required  He/CH 4 selectivity  of  the  membrane  to  undertake  the  separation  decreases,  which  is  expected  because  the driving force for the separation increases. Similarly, as the feed pressure increases the required He/CH 4 selectivity decreases for the same helium feed composition, again because the driving force through the membrane increases. For the two membrane stages to achieve a purity of 99%, the He/CH4 selectivity must  be  at  least  54,  for  a  feed  of  4  mol%  He  and  at  10  MPa  (Figure  4).  This  He/CH 4 selectivity  is

texts.orig: The membranes' stage-cut are an indication of the operating conditions of the direct helium recovery from natural  gas  processes.  The  second  membrane stage-cut  is  provided  in  Figure  5  as  a  function  of He/CH4 selectivity and various feed compositions, where the feed gas is 10 MPa. Other feed pressures have similar stage-cut selectivity trends. As the helium concentration in the feed reduces, the second membrane's  stage-cut  is  also  reduced  because  the  recycle  stream  between  the  first  and  second membrane stages has a progressively lower He concentration. As such, an increasingly smaller fraction of this recycle must be separated through the second membrane to ensure 99% product purity. For a feed gas of 0.05 mol% He or less, the second membrane's stage-cut is at most 0.05, for high membrane's selectivity, which represents marginal operating conditions. The decrease in stage-cut with decreasing He/CH4  selectivity  is  for  similar  reasons,  the  lower  selectivity  results  in  dilution  of  He  in  the  recycle between the first  and  second  membrane  stages,  as  such  a  decreasing  fraction  of  this  recycle  can  be separated  by  the  second  membrane  stage  to  ensure  product  purity.  Hence,  the  minimum  He/CH 4 selectivity needed to achieve the desired separation requires the overall process to operate at very low stage-cuts for the second membrane stage. The performance of the two membrane stage process for a 5 MPa feed pressure are provided in Table 2, to highlight the minimum He/CH4 selectivity required to achieve desired purity and recovery, as well as stage-cut in the first membrane and overall energy duty of the process.

texts.orig: The He/CH4 selectivity required for three membrane stages in series to achieve 99% purity product from natural gas of various helium concentration is provided in Figure 6, as a function of feed pressure and pressure driving force. As this process has two recycle streams, 99 % helium recovery is also achieved. Similar  to  the  previous  process  design,  increasing  the  feed  helium  composition  and  overall  pressure across  the  membrane  results  in  a  decrease  in  the  required  He/CH 4 selectivity  needed  to  achieve  the separation.  The  three  membrane  stages  reduced  the  required  selectivity  relative  two  membranes process,  because  of  the  use  of  two  recycle  streams.  For  a  feed  stream  of  4  mol%  and  10  MPa,  the minimum  He/CH4  selectivity  to  achieve  separation  is  25,  which  is  achievable  for  a  wide  range  of polymeric  membranes  (Figure  3).  Decreasing  the  composition  in  the  feed,  the  cut  off  for  applying current polymeric membrane technology is ~0.6 mol% at 10 MPa,  0.7 mol% field at 7.5 MPa, 0.8 mol% field at 5 MPa and a 1.1 mol% field at 2.5 MPa. When compared to the two membrane stage process, the addition of a third stage results in only a minor reduction in the helium compositions that can be successfully  separated  based  on  current  membranes.  The  membranes'  stage  cuts  in  the  three membrane  process  being  increasingly  marginal  as  the  He/CH 4 selectivity  is  reduced,  similar  to  that observed in the two membrane process (Figure 5). This is because reduced selectivity results in the two recycle  streams,  between the  first  and  second  membranes  as  well  as  between  the  second  and  third membranes,  having  lower  He  concentrations.  As  a  result,  the  stage-cut  in  the  second  and  third membranes become increasingly  smaller.  For  example,  for  feed  composition  of  1  mol%  He  and  at  5 MPa,  the  first  membrane  stage-cut  is  0.2,  second  membrane  stage-cut  is  0.12  and  third  membrane stage-cut is 0.01, at the lowest viable selectivity of 160. The performance of the three membrane stage process for 5 MPa feed pressure are provided in Table 2, to highlight the minimum He/CH4 selectivity required, as well as other process conditions.

texts.orig: The  pressure  ratio  across  the  membrane  stages  in  the  two  and  three  membrane  processes  can  be altered, to improve the separation performance and energy duty of the process. To achieve the lowest energy  duty  possible,  the  feed  gas  pressure  drop  is  staggered  equally  through  the  two  and  three membrane stages processes. This ensures that the only compression required is for the recycle streams, and that there is no compression on the feed streams to the respective membranes. For example, for 10 MPa feed and two membranes in stages, each membrane experiences a 4.95 MPa pressure driving force with the final permeate at 0.1 MPa and for a three membranes in stages, each membrane experiences 3.3 MPa pressure driving force. As a result the pressure drop across the individual membrane stages is lower  and  therefore  possible  plasticization  of  the  polymeric  membrane  is  reduced.  Staggering  the pressure  drop  through  the  process  increases  the  necessary  He/CH 4 selectivity  to  achieve  99%  purity product,  shown  in  Figures  6  and  7,  because  the  driving  force  in  the  individual  membrane  stages  is significantly reduced. For a 4 mol% feed and 10 MPa, the required He/CH4 selectivity must be above 170 for three membrane stages and 245 for two membrane stages. These selectivities are currently possible with membranes based on poly vinyl fluoride, Trithene B and polypyrrolone [8, 17]. However, it is clear for both two and three membrane stages that for a feed helium composition of less than 1 mol% current membranes  are  not  viable  in  this  process,  because  of  the  significant  increase  in  required  He/CH 4 selectivity,  especially  when  the  feed  pressure  is  less  than  7.5  MPa.  The  energy  duty  for  these  two process designs ranges from 4 to 10 MJ/kg of He recovered, dependent on feed composition, pressure

texts.orig: Natural gas fields may also have N 2 present and this will impact on the behaviour reported here. This is because N2 generally, but not always, has higher permeance than CH4 in polymeric membranes [17] and the N2 flux across the membrane dilutes the He concentration of the permeate stream. To counter this dilution,  less  CH 4 must  permeate  through  the  membrane  and  hence  a  higher  He/CH4  selectivity  is required. The increase in the He/CH 4 selectivity required is heavily dependent on the N 2 concentration in the feed gas and the feed N2 to He ratio. Hence, based on these process designs, direct separation and purification  of  helium  from  natural  gas  fields  through  only  gas  separation  membranes  is  potentially possible with currently available polymeric membranes, but it is limited to those natural gas fields with relatively high helium concentration and at considerably pressure.

texts.text: 3.2 Helium recovery from nitrogen

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For an existing helium recovery process that is part of a liquefied natural gas process,  gas separation membranes can be incorporated into  the  process  after  the  NRU,  where  membranes  can  be  used  to recovery and concentrate the initial NRU product gas from 1 - 3 mol% to 70%, as well as to upgrade the He  composition  to  90%.  Both  of  these  processes  require  the  membrane  to  separate  helium  from nitrogen,  rather  than  methane.  A  number  of  polymeric  membranes  have  been  reported  for  helium separation from nitrogen [8, 17-19, 23], and the Robeson's plot of this separation is provided in Figure 9. All membranes  display  selectivity for helium, with the upper bound  highlighting the current performance, again with the gradient corresponding to theory [21].

texts.orig: A single membrane stage can recovery the NRU crude exit gas and concentrate up to 70 % with a He/N2 selectivity  greater  than  5  (which  a  recovery  of  99%),  dependent  on  the  total  pressure  across  the membrane  to  generate  the  required  driving  force.  The  relationship  between  He/N 2 selectivity  and membrane pressure difference (MPa) is provided in Figure 10, as a function of helium concentration in the  feed  gas.  As  the  He/N 2 selectivity  of  the  membrane  increases  the  required  pressure  across  the membrane decreases as the necessary separation can be achieved with a lower driving force. Similarly, increasing the concentration of helium in the feed from the NRU decreases the required pressure. For a He/N2 selectivity of 10, 1 mol % He in the feed requires a pressure driving force of 40 MPa for recovery and concentration of helium to 70 mol%, while for a 2 mol % He feed the necessary pressure is 18 MPa, and for 3 mol% it is 15.5 MPa. These are significantly high pressures for a single membrane process, which will give rise to membrane plasticization as well as requiring significant feed compression.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recovery helium from the NRU exit gas will be better achieved with a two membrane stages process, which requires a more reasonable pressure driving forces across the membrane, as shown in Figure 11. This separation can be achieved through a membrane with He/N2 selectivity 5 or higher. Importantly, there  is  a  clear  transition  in  the  relationship  between  selectivity  and  pressure  for  the  three  feed conditions studied, where the performance of the membrane process changes dramatically. For a feed composition of 3 mol%, this transition occurs at a He/N 2 selectivity of 25, for 2 mol% feed a selectivity of 30 and for 1 mol% a selectivity of 80. The performance of the process at this transition point is provided in Table 3, and for a 3 mol% feed this selectivity is achievable with a range of polymeric membranes, such as those based on polyimides, poly vinyl fluoride and Nylon 6. Operating the two membrane stages process  at  these  selectivities  or  higher  requires  a  relatively  low  pressure  driving  force  across  the membranes.  Operating  the  two  membranes  process  at  selectivities  below  these  and  the  necessary pressure  driving  force  across  both  membranes  increases  exponentially  as  a  function  of  decreasing selectivity. This is because the separation becomes harder to achieve at these low selectivites and the membrane  process  must  operate  at  marginal  conditions  to  achieve  the  required  recovery.  This  is highlighted by the change to stage-cut, which are provided in Figure 12 for the first membrane. Above the transition, the second membrane process operates at a stage-cut of 0.41 to 0.43 for 3 mol% feed, 0.36 to 0.38 for 2 mol% and 0.3 for 1 mol%. Below the transition in selectivity operation, the stage-cut of the  first  membrane  stage  dramatically  decreases  with  decreasing  He/N 2 selectivity,  because  of  the significant amount of N2 recycled back from the second membrane stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Importantly,  the  selectivity  of  currently  available  polymeric  membranes  (Figure  9)  enables  the  two membrane stages process to be operated above the transition point, irrespective of feed composition. Hence, gas separation membranes are viable in recovering helium from the NRU exit gas. However, the necessary pressure driving force requires compression of both membrane stages' feed stream, which required an energy duty of 6.6 to 14.1 MJ/kg of He recovered, dependent on the membrane selectivity and feed composition. These are comparable to that observed in the helium separation from natural gas process (Section 3.1.1), and are high relative to traditional helium recovery technologies [1]. The low exit pressure from the NRU means that compression is required in this process and hence this energy duty cannot  be  avoided.  For  selectivities  below  the  transition  point,  the  exponential  increase  in  pressure driving force gives rise to an exponential increase in compression energy duty, which becomes an order of magnitude greater than that observed for selectivities above the transitions.

texts.orig: Applying three membrane stages to recovery helium from the NRU and concentrate to 70 mol% reduces the pressure driving force needed across the membrane stages, as shown in Figure 13 as a function of membrane selectivity and feed composition. The minimum selectivity to achieve this helium recovery can  be  achieved  with  a  He/N 2 selectivity  of  5;  however  the  pressure  driving  force  required  is considerable.  Again,  there  is  a  transition  in  the  selectivity  -  pressure  relationship  which  indicated  a dramatic  change  in  how  the  membrane  process  operates,  and  details  are  provided  in  Table  3.  For  a three membrane stages in series feed composition of 3 mol%, the transition is at He/N 2 selectivity of 20, for  2  mol%  feed  it  is  at  selectivity  of  22,  and  for  1  mol%  at  a  selectivity  of  30.  Again,  operating  the process with membranes above these selectivites required only mild membrane pressure driving force. A number of polymeric membranes have He/N2 selectivities greater than these, such as polyvinylidene chloride and Hyflon AD60. Utilizing membranes with selectivites below the transition and the membrane process  becomes  marginal  to  achieve  the  required  recovery  and  purity,  giving  rise  to  an  exponential increase in the pressure driving force with decreasing selectivity. Similarly, the behaviour in stage-cut follows the same trend as that reported for the two membrane process, in that operating at selectivities below  the  transition  point  results  in  decreasing  stage-cut  and  more  marginal  operating  conditions. Importantly, selectivties above the transition point exist with current polymeric membranes (Figure 9) and the necessary pressure driving forces are achievable, with an energy duty of 12.1 to 33 MJ/kg of He recovered, dependent on the membrane selectivity and feed composition.
texts.text: Applying three membrane stages to recovery helium from the NRU and concentrate to 70 mol% reduces the pressure driving force needed across the membrane stages, as shown in Figure 13 as a function of membrane selectivity and feed composition. The minimum selectivity to achieve this helium recovery can  be  achieved  with  a  He/N 2 selectivity  of  5;  however  the  pressure  driving  force  required  is considerable.  Again,  there  is  a  transition  in  the  selectivity  -  pressure  relationship  which  indicated  a dramatic  change  in  how  the  membrane  process  operates,  and  details  are  provided  in  Table  3.  For  a three membrane stages in series feed composition of 3 mol%, the transition is at He/N 2 selectivity of 20, for  2  mol%  feed  it  is  at  selectivity  of  22,  and  for  1  mol%  at  a  selectivity  of  30.  Again,  operating  the process with membranes above these selectivites required only mild membrane pressure driving force. A number of polymeric membranes have He/N2 selectivities greater than these, such as polyvinylidene chloride and Hyflon AD60. Utilizing membranes with selectivites below the transition and the membrane process  becomes  marginal  to  achieve  the  required  recovery  and  purity,  giving  rise  to  an  exponential increase in the pressure driving force with decreasing selectivity. Similarly, the behaviour in stage-cut follows the same trend as that reported for the two membrane process, in that operating at selectivities below  the  transition  point  results  in  decreasing  stage-cut  and  more  marginal  operating  conditions. Importantly, selectivties above the transition point exist with current polymeric membranes (Figure 9) and the necessary pressure driving forces are achievable, with an energy duty of 12.1 to 33 MJ/kg of He recovered, dependent on the membrane selectivity and feed composition.

However,  the  compression  duty  required  is  considerable  and  will influence the feasibility relative to traditional recovery technologies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.2 Helium upgrading to 90% purity
texts.text: 3.2.2 Helium upgrading to 90% purity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Upgrading  the  50  -  70%  helium  product  to  90%  required  for  subsequent  purification  by  PSA  can  be achieved  through  a  membrane  process.  For  a  single  membrane  stage  to  undertake  this  purification (irrespective of helium recovery), the necessary He/N 2 selectivity as a function of feed composition and membrane pressure driving force is provided in Figure 14. Decreasing the selectivity of the membrane results  in  an  increased  pressure  driving  force  required  to  generate  the  separation,  with  the  pressure increase substantially below a selectivity of 10 for a 70 mol% feed, a selectivity of 12 for a 60 mol% feed and a selectivity of 20 for a 50 mol% feed. It is necessary to generate this pressure driving force through feed compressors, as the exit gas from the helium recovery stage is close to atmospheric conditions. Importantly,  the  selectivities  corresponding  to  reasonably  low  pressure  driving  forces  are  achievable with existing commercial membrane materials (Figure 9).

Importantly,  the  selectivities  corresponding  to  reasonably  low  pressure  driving  forces  are  achievable with existing commercial membrane materials (Figure 9).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Upgrading the helium product to 90 mol% can be achieved with two membrane stages with a recovery of 99%, reducing the pressure driving force across the membrane for a constant selectivity relative to the single stage process, as shown in Figure 15. Again, with reducing membrane He/N2 selectivity the pressure  driving  force  across  both  membrane  units  increases  substantially;  most  evident  below  a selectivity of 7 for a 70 mol% feed, a selectivity of 10 for a 60 mol% feed and a selectivity of 17 for a 50 mol% feed. The need to compress the feed stream to both membrane stages requires an energy duty of 1.5 - 2.4 MJ/kg of He recovered, dependent on the feed composition and pressure, where the recycle ratio  is  between  0.6  -  0.8  of  the  feed  stream.  Again,  these  membrane  selectivites  and  pressure conditions are achievable based on current polymeric membranes performance (Figure 9). The stagecuts  required  are  also  reasonable,  with  the  first  membrane  having  a  stage-cut  of  0.91  to  0.96, dependent on membrane selectivity, feed pressure and He mol%, while the second membrane requires a stage-cut of 0.17 to 0.079, again dependent on selectivity, feed pressure and composition. texts.orig: the  different  helium  separation  stages  investigated  here,  the  upgrading  to  90%  process  is  the  most feasible  based  on  membrane  selectivity  and  compression  energy  compared  to  traditional  separation processes. Applying a three membrane stages process reduces the pressure driving force further and enables even lower membrane He/N2 selectivities to become viable, however the need to have three compressor  stages  (for  the  feed  stream  of  each  membrane  unit)  results  in  the  energy  duty  of  the process  increasing. texts.orig: Applying gas separation membranes to the final helium purification stage is difficult, because of the very high purities needed and the presence of trace amount of hydrogen. It is clear that PSA is ideally suited for this purification stage and polymeric membranes very limited as a potential alternative separation technology.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas  separation  membrane  processes  are  simulated  here  for  the  recovery  and  purification  of  helium from  natural  gas,  in  terms  of  direct  recovery  from  natural  gas  and  incorporated  as  part  of  helium recovery from the exit gas of a NRU. It was determined that for direct recovery and purification from natural gas, there exists current polymeric membranes that have the necessary high He/CH4 selectivity to  undertake  the  separation  for  natural  gas  fields  with  helium  composition  of  1  mol%  or  higher. However  the  viability  of  the  process  is  questionable  given  the  high  compression  duties  required  to generate the pressure driving force across the membranes stages to achieve the level of separation. For recovery of helium from the NRU exit gas, there exists clear transitions in the necessary He/N 2 selectivity of the membrane. Utilizing membranes with selectivities below the transition point resulted in marginal operating conditions and an exponential increase in the pressure driving force for decreasing membrane selectivity. The most viable process was the upgrading of helium to 90% from the recovery stage, which could  be  undertaken  by  a  two  membrane  stages  process  with  reasonable  He/N 2 selectivities  and compression  duty  comparable  to  current  separation  technologies.  It  is  clear  for  all  the  separation processes  studied  here  that  improving  the  He/CH 4 and/or  He/N 2 selectivity  of  polymeric  membranes increases the separation performance. However, only for direct separation of helium from natural gas was there a clear benefit in major improvements in selectivity above existing membranes, as it would
